prompt:
Airport: KJFK (John F. Kennedy International, New York). Hot Spot HS-1: 'Twy K and Twy J complex intersection close proximity to Rwy 04L and Rwy 31L.'

Situation: Aircraft AAL501 (B747-400) has been cleared 'taxi to runway 04L via Taxiway K.' The crew is unfamiliar with KJFK. As AAL501 approaches the Twy K / Twy J intersection, the crew is uncertain which taxiway segment is K and which is J, and both appear to lead toward runway 04L. Current time: 07:15Z, day VMC, wind 040/12.

Identify all surface movement hazards in this situation.
gold_decision:
Three hazards identified:
1. HOT SPOT HS-1 DISORIENTATION: KJFK HS-1 specifically describes the K/J complex intersection as having close proximity to both Rwy 04L and Rwy 31L. Crew confusion about which taxiway is K vs J may lead AAL501 to inadvertently enter the wrong taxiway, potentially turning toward Rwy 31L instead of tracking toward Rwy 04L. In a B747-400 (wingspan ~211 ft), maneuvering in this tight intersection carries additional risk due to wingspan clearances — KJFK prohibits aircraft with wingspan >140 ft on Twy A or B bridges.
2. RUNWAY 31L PROXIMITY RISK: The hot spot description states proximity to both Rwy 04L and Rwy 31L. If AAL501 follows the wrong segment of the K/J intersection, the crew could approach Rwy 31L without intending to — requiring an immediate stop and ATC query before any runway entry.
3. SPEED AND WINGSPAN: KJFK requires max 17 kts/20 mph on all taxiways for B747-400 class. In an unfamiliar complex intersection, reduced taxi speed is critical to allow time to verify taxiway identity against the airport diagram before committing to a turn.

Recommendation: Stop at the K/J intersection, verify exact position against KJFK airport diagram, confirm with ground control which taxiway segment to take, and proceed only after positive identification of Taxiway K.

KJFK max taxi speed 17 kts/20 mph for B747-400 class aircraft on all taxiways (FAA Chart Supplement NE, cycle 20260514, KJFK, p.226)
Crew must verify taxiway identity against current airport diagram before proceeding at complex intersections (FAA AC 91-73B §3.4)

prompt:
Airport: KLGA (LaGuardia, New York). Hot Spot HS-2: 'Maintain vigilance when exiting Rwy 04 at Twy Q, crossing onto another twy.'

Situation: Aircraft UAL888 (B737-800) has just landed on Rwy 04, decelerating through the touchdown zone. Ground control has cleared UAL888: 'Exit via Twy Q, taxi to gate B12.' As UAL888 exits at Twy Q, the crew observes that the taxiway geometry near HS-2 creates an ambiguous junction where Twy Q transitions into another taxiway. KLGA speed limit on Twy DD, Z, B, and Twy AA is 10 kts. Weather: 10SM CLR, wind 040/08.

Identify all surface movement hazards.
gold_decision:
Two hazards identified:
1. HOT SPOT HS-2 EXIT CONFUSION: KLGA HS-2 specifically calls out vigilance when exiting Rwy 04 at Twy Q because exiting onto another taxiway can cause inadvertent wrong-taxiway entry. UAL888 decelerating and turning onto Twy Q must verify they are on Twy Q and not mistakenly continuing movement onto an adjacent taxiway at the junction. A crew distracted by post-landing checklist at this critical exit point may follow the wrong path.
2. SPEED COMPLIANCE ON RESTRICTED TAXIWAYS: KLGA has a 10-knot speed limit on Twy DD, Z, B, and Twy AA. If UAL888's route to gate B12 uses any of these taxiways, crew must reduce to 10 kts. After a landing roll-out, managing deceleration to comply with the 10-knot limit on named taxiways is a specific operational constraint.

Recommendation: UAL888 should slow to walking pace when exiting at Twy Q, verify exact position against KLGA airport diagram, confirm Twy Q alignment before turning, and monitor for the 10-knot speed limit taxiways on the route to B12.

prompt:
Airport: KBOS (Boston Logan International). Hot Spot HS-3: 'Twy E and Twy K approaching Rwy 04L-22R.'

Situation: Aircraft JBU344 (A320) is taxiing outbound for departure on Rwy 22R. Ground cleared: 'JBU344, taxi to runway 22R via Taxiway E, Taxiway K.' JBU344 is now approaching the HS-3 area. A second aircraft, EJA901 (Citation X), is cleared to hold short of Rwy 22R via Taxiway K from the opposite direction. Weather: 5SM BR, OVC008, wind 220/09.

Identify all surface movement hazards.
gold_decision:
Three hazards identified:
1. HOT SPOT HS-3 — RUNWAY PROXIMITY: KBOS HS-3 identifies Twy E and Twy K approaching Rwy 04L-22R as a high-vigilance zone. Both taxiways converge near the runway, and in reduced visibility (5SM with mist, OVC 800ft), the crew must exercise extreme care to identify the runway holding position markings and not overshoot the hold-short line.
2. OPPOSING TRAFFIC ON SHARED TAXIWAY: JBU344 on Twy K (outbound) and EJA901 on Twy K from the opposite direction creates a head-on conflict potential on a taxiway that already has HS-3 proximity risk. If both aircraft enter the hot spot area simultaneously from opposite directions, maneuvering room is restricted.
3. LOW VISIBILITY WITH RUNWAY APPROACH: OVC008 / 5SM BR puts operations near SVFR weather minimums. In these conditions, KBOS would likely have enhanced surface movement precautions active. The crew at HS-3 must slow significantly, scan for the runway holding position markings, and confirm the hold-short position before reaching the runway.

Recommendation: ATC should sequence JBU344 and EJA901 to avoid simultaneous presence at HS-3; JBU344 crew should significantly reduce taxi speed approaching the K/E intersection in reduced visibility.

prompt:
Airport: KPHL (Philadelphia International). Hot Spot HS-1: 'Tfc taxiing south on Twy Y must not miss turn at Twy S onto Rwy 09R-27L.'

Situation: Aircraft AAL292 (A321) is cleared to taxi southbound on Twy Y, then turn onto Twy S to hold short of Rwy 27L. It is night VMC. The crew is fatigued after a long duty day. As AAL292 proceeds south on Twy Y, the Twy S turnoff is approaching. Weather: 10SM CLR, wind 270/15.

Identify the surface movement hazards.
gold_decision:
Two hazards identified:
1. HOT SPOT HS-1 MISSED TURN: KPHL HS-1 specifically warns that aircraft taxiing south on Twy Y must not miss the Twy S turn — this miss leads directly onto Rwy 09R-27L. If AAL292's fatigued crew fails to identify the Twy S turnoff, AAL292 will enter the runway without clearance. At night, the Twy S junction may be less visually prominent than during daylight, increasing the missed-turn risk.
2. NIGHT OPERATIONS COMPOUNDING FATIGUE: Nighttime reduces visual runway environment cues. Runway holding position markings and guard lights are the primary cues to stop — if the crew has missed the Twy S turn and reaches the runway holding position, they must stop there. Fatigue is a contributing factor in runway incursions (documented in FAA runway safety incident data).

Recommendation: Crew should slow to minimum taxi speed at the HS-1 area, both crew members should monitor the airport diagram and call out the Twy S turn point, and crew should verify position before committing south past the Twy S junction.

Crew fatigue is a runway safety risk factor requiring heightened cross-check procedures (FAA AC 91-73B §2.1)

prompt:
Airport: KATL (Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International). Hot Spot HS-1: 'Intersections of Twy C and Twy D at Rwy 08L-26R; hold short line 250 ft after exiting FBO ramp; canted hold bar.'

Situation: Aircraft DAL1501 (B757-200) has departed from the FBO ramp and is taxiing toward Rwy 26R for departure. The crew was briefed on HS-1. As DAL1501 exits the FBO ramp onto Twy C, the crew sees a hold bar markings on the pavement. The hold bar appears to be angled (canted) relative to the taxiway centerline. Weather: 8SM SCT040, wind 260/12.

Identify surface movement hazards.
gold_decision:
Two hazards identified:
1. HOT SPOT HS-1 CANTED HOLD BAR CONFUSION: KATL HS-1 specifically warns about a canted (angled) hold bar 250 ft after exiting the FBO ramp. A canted hold bar is non-perpendicular to the aircraft's travel path, which can cause crews to misjudge the hold position — some crews inadvertently cross the canted hold bar because it does not look like a standard perpendicular runway holding position marking. The HS-1 description confirms this is a documented confusion point at KATL Rwy 08L-26R.
2. PROXIMITY TO RWY 08L-26R: After exiting the FBO ramp, DAL1501 is on a taxiway that leads to the Rwy 08L-26R holding position within 250 ft — a very short distance. At normal taxi speed of 15-20 kts, the crew has only seconds from ramp exit to reach the hold bar. The 'canted' geometry means standard perpendicular hold-bar visual pattern recognition does not apply.

Recommendation: After exiting the FBO ramp, immediately slow to minimum speed, actively look for the canted hold bar markings per the KATL airport diagram, and stop at those markings. Do not proceed onto Rwy 08L-26R without explicit ATC clearance.
required_safety_constraints: KATL HS-1: hold bar 250 ft after FBO ramp exit at Twy C/D intersection with Rwy 08L-26R; canted hold bar geometry (FAA Chart Supplement SE, cycle 20260514, FAA_CS_SE_20260514.pdf, p.603)
Canted hold bars must be identified by their pavement markings, not by their angle relative to the aircraft heading (FAA AC 150/5340-1L §2.17)
Maximum taxi speed on Twy A/D between Twy SC/Twy P at KATL is 15 mph (FAA Chart Supplement SE, cycle 20260514, KATL)

prompt:
Airport: KATL (Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International). LAHSO operation: 'Rwy 08L hold short Twy B13 (available landing distance 8490 ft).'

Situation: Aircraft AAL404 (B737-800, MLW ~144,000 lbs) has accepted a Land-and-Hold-Short-Operations (LAHSO) clearance to land Rwy 08L and hold short of Twy B13. AAL404 is on a 2-mile final. The crew has confirmed the available landing distance of 8490 ft is sufficient for their dry-runway MLW stopping performance. However, tower then reports: 'Winds 090/12 with gusts to 22.' The crew re-calculates: at MLW with a 10-knot tailwind component the stopping distance increases significantly.

Identify the surface movement hazards and required action.
gold_decision:
Two critical hazards identified:
1. LAHSO DISTANCE INSUFFICIENCY IN GUSTY CONDITIONS: The crew accepted LAHSO for 8490 ft available landing distance based on initial conditions. Updated wind 090/12 gusting to 22 means a potential tailwind component on Rwy 08L (runway heading 080°). Even a 5-knot effective tailwind at landing increases stopping distance by 10-15% for a B737-800 at MLW. Combined with gust factor, stopping distance for the LAHSO maneuver may now be insufficient to hold short of Twy B13.
2. MANDATORY LAHSO REJECTION: If the updated stopping distance analysis shows the LAHSO available distance is insufficient, the crew MUST reject the LAHSO clearance and request a full-length landing. Per FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-10-8, a pilot may reject a LAHSO clearance at any time up to the final approach fix (FAA). Proceeding with an accepted LAHSO clearance when stopping distance may be insufficient risks a runway exceedance into Twy B13.

Required action: Crew must inform ATC 'unable LAHSO' and request full-length landing on Rwy 08L, allowing ATC to coordinate with any crossing traffic at Twy B13.
required_safety_constraints: LAHSO clearance must be rejected if updated wind/weight analysis shows available landing distance may be insufficient (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-10-8)
KATL Rwy 08L LAHSO hold short point is Twy B13, available landing distance 8490 ft (FAA Chart Supplement SE, cycle 20260514, FAA_CS_SE_20260514.pdf, p.204)
Crew may reject LAHSO up to the FAF — there is no penalty for unable-LAHSO declaration (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-10-8)
ATC must sequence crossing traffic at Twy B13 after LAHSO rejection — immediate ATC coordination required
acceptable_variants: Crew may also continue if updated calculation confirms adequate stopping margin with gust factor — but the decision must be actively re-evaluated, not assumed based on initial acceptance
evidence_requirements: FAA Chart Supplement Southeast U.S., cycle 20260514, FAA_CS_SE_20260514.pdf, p.204 — KATL LAHSO data

prompt:
Airport: KCLT (Charlotte Douglas International). Hot Spot HS-2: 'Pilots exiting Rwy 18C-36C on Twy S for Twy E or Twy F mistakenly turn left on Twy E6.'

Situation: Aircraft CLT220 (CRJ-900) has just landed on Rwy 36C. Ground control has cleared: 'CLT220, exit via Twy S, proceed to gate C16 via Twy F.' As CLT220 vacates at Twy S and begins heading toward Twy F, the crew sees two possible left-turn taxiway options ahead: Twy F and Twy E6. The signage in this area is the subject of the HS-2 warning. Weather: 3SM -RA OVC012.

Identify all surface movement hazards.
gold_decision:
Two hazards identified:
1. HOT SPOT HS-2 WRONG TAXIWAY ENTRY: KCLT HS-2 documents that crews exiting Rwy 18C-36C on Twy S for Twy E or Twy F frequently turn left on Twy E6 instead. In reduced visibility (3SM rain, OVC 1200ft), the wrong-taxiway risk at HS-2 is elevated because visual cues are degraded. CLT220's crew must positively identify the Twy F turnoff using airport diagram reference, not just visual scanning.
2. LOW VISIBILITY COMPOUNDING NAVIGATION ERROR: At 3SM/-RA/OVC012, the KCLT ground environment is significantly degraded from normal VMC. Taxiway junction signs may be harder to read. A wrong turn onto Twy E6 could bring CLT220 into an unintended area of the airfield that may be in proximity to other runways or movement areas.

Recommendation: CLT220 should slow to minimum taxi speed at Twy S, reference the KCLT airport diagram explicitly, identify Twy F signage (yellow on black taxiway location sign), and NOT turn until positively identifying Twy F. If uncertain, stop and query ATC.

prompt:
Airport: KLAX (Los Angeles International). Hot Spot HS-1: 'Rwy 24L at Twys V, W, Y — short distance between parallel rwys; after landing Rwy 24L, manage taxi speed, hold short Rwy 24R at appropriate rwy holding position markings.'

Situation: Aircraft UAL562 (B777-200, wingspan ~200 ft) has just landed on Rwy 24L. After touchdown, ground control issues: 'UAL562, exit via Twy V, hold short of Rwy 24R.' UAL562 is decelerating through the exit zone. The crew notes that Twys V, W, Y are all available exits in close succession. Weather: 10SM CLR, wind 250/10.

Identify all surface movement hazards.
gold_decision:
Three hazards identified:
1. HOT SPOT HS-1 SHORT PARALLEL DISTANCE: KLAX HS-1 specifically warns that the distance between Rwy 24L and Rwy 24R is short at Twys V, W, Y. A B777-200 exiting at Twy V, W, or Y will reach the Rwy 24R holding position very quickly after exiting. If the crew manages taxi speed poorly (too fast), they may overshoot the Rwy 24R holding position, especially while still decelerating from landing speed.
2. WINGSPAN CONSTRAINT: KLAX prohibits simultaneous aircraft operations on Twy L and H9 between Rwys 07L-25R and Rwy 07R-25L, and has wingspan restrictions on taxilanes C7, C8, C9 for >240 ft wingspan. UAL562's B777-200 at ~200 ft wingspan requires the crew to verify routing is clear. Twy V/W/Y toward Rwy 24R holds must be checked for wingspan restrictions on these high-traffic taxiways.
3. HOLD SHORT COMPLIANCE AT RWY 24R: Active departures on Rwy 24R while UAL562 decelerates from landing creates a critical timing window. The crew must stop before the Rwy 24R holding position marking. A high-speed overshoot into Rwy 24R during an active departure sequence is a Category A incursion risk.

Recommendation: Exit at the earliest available taxiway (V, W, or Y per ATC instruction), aggressively manage deceleration to stop well before the Rwy 24R hold-short marking.
required_safety_constraints: KLAX HS-1: after landing Rwy 24L, manage taxi speed and hold short of Rwy 24R at V, W, Y exits — short parallel runway distance (FAA Chart Supplement SW, cycle 20260514, FAA_CS_SW_20260514.pdf, p.646)
No simultaneous ops on Twy L and H9 between Rwy 07L-25R and 07R-25L at KLAX (FAA Chart Supplement SW, cycle 20260514, FAA_CS_SW_20260514.pdf, p.200)
Aircraft must hold short of Rwy 24R until explicit ATC crossing clearance received (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2)
Taxi speed must allow confident stop at any hold-short position on the route (FAA AC 91-73B §3.1)

prompt:
Airport: KLAX (Los Angeles International). Hot Spot HS-3: 'Twy F from Twy A — ATC frequently instructs hold short of Twy F at intermediate holding position; do not proceed onto Twy F without ATC clearance.'

Situation: Aircraft SWA1701 (B737-MAX8) has been instructed: 'SWA1701, taxi via Twy A, hold short of Twy F.' SWA1701 is northbound on Twy A approaching the Twy F intersection. The crew sees an intermediate holding position marking on the pavement before the Twy F centerline. Twy F appears clear of traffic. Weather: 10SM CLR.

Identify the surface movement hazard.
gold_decision:
Critical hazard identified:
1. INTERMEDIATE HOLDING POSITION HS-3: KLAX HS-3 specifically documents that ATC frequently issues hold short of Twy F at an intermediate holding position on Twy A — not at the Twy F centerline. The intermediate holding position marking (double solid yellow lines before the intersection) is the correct stop point. If SWA1701's crew stops at the Twy F centerline instead of at the intermediate hold position, they may be blocking Twy F traffic or improperly positioned.
2. NO-PROCEED-WITHOUT-CLEARANCE RULE: The HS-3 description explicitly states 'do not proceed onto Twy F without ATC clearance.' This means even if Twy F looks clear, SWA1701 must hold at the intermediate position and wait for ATC to explicitly clear them onto Twy F. Proceeding without clearance is a taxiway incursion risk given the complex movement geometry at this intersection.

Required action: SWA1701 must stop at the intermediate holding position marking (before the Twy F centerline), not at the centerline itself, and await explicit ATC clearance to proceed onto Twy F.
required_safety_constraints: KLAX HS-3: hold short of Twy F at intermediate holding position on Twy A; do not proceed onto Twy F without ATC clearance (FAA Chart Supplement SW, cycle 20260514, FAA_CS_SW_20260514.pdf, p.646)
Intermediate holding positions are mandatory stop points when instructed by ATC (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2)
Twy F appearance of being clear does not authorize proceed — only explicit ATC clearance does (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2)
KLAX has ASDE-X surface detection — unauthorized movement onto Twy F will be observed by ATC

prompt:
Airport: KSFO (San Francisco International). Hot Spot HS-3: 'Acft exiting Rwy 28R on Twy T; taxi acft up to and hold short of Rwy 28L unless specifically cleared to cross; Twy T has hold short lines for both Rwy 28R and 28L; aircraft tail must be fully clear of Rwy 28L.'

Situation: Aircraft UAL873 (B777-300ER, overall length ~242 ft) has just landed on Rwy 28R and is exiting via Twy T. Ground control has cleared: 'UAL873, hold short of Rwy 28L.' UAL873 stops with the nose at the Rwy 28L holding position marking. Is this correct?

Identify the surface movement hazard.
gold_decision:
Critical hazard identified:
1. TAIL CLEARANCE RISK — NOSE AT HOLD LINE IS INSUFFICIENT: KSFO HS-3 explicitly states 'aircraft tail must be fully clear of Rwy 28L.' UAL873 is a B777-300ER with an overall length of ~242 ft. If UAL873 stops with its NOSE at the Rwy 28L holding position marking, the aircraft's tail extends approximately 242 ft behind the nose — which means the tail is still physically on or over the Rwy 28R surface (or the Rwy 28R-28L taxi area). The crew must advance far enough forward that the ENTIRE aircraft (including tail) clears Rwy 28R AND positions the nose correctly behind the Rwy 28L hold-short marking.
2. BOTH RUNWAY HOLD LINES: Twy T has hold short lines for both Rwy 28R and Rwy 28L. UAL873 must first pass the Rwy 28R hold line (exiting the runway), then advance to the position where nose is behind the Rwy 28L hold line AND the tail is clear of Rwy 28R. For a 242-ft aircraft, this requires careful position management.

Required action: UAL873 crew must ensure the entire aircraft has cleared Rwy 28R before stopping at the Rwy 28L hold line — verify tail clearance before stopping.
required_safety_constraints: KSFO HS-3: aircraft tail must be fully clear of Rwy 28L when holding short on Twy T (FAA Chart Supplement SW, cycle 20260514, FAA_CS_SW_20260514.pdf, p.647)
For long aircraft, nose-at-hold-line does not guarantee tail clearance of the previous runway — crew must verify (FAA AC 91-73B §3.6)

prompt:
Airport: KSFO (San Francisco International). Hot Spot HS-4: 'ATC may request hold on RWY short of intersection with another RWY; at rwy intersections, vertical holding position signs exist with no surface painted markings; when instructed, pilots must hold at the holding position signs.'

Situation: Aircraft SWA3301 (B737-800) is on Rwy 28R, cleared for takeoff from the full length. After beginning the takeoff roll, ATC issues: 'SWA3301, hold — hold position!' SWA3301 is approaching an intersection with another runway at midfield. The crew sees vertical holding position signs but NO painted surface markings at this intersection. Weather: 10SM CLR.

Identify the surface movement hazard and required action.
gold_decision:
Critical hazard identified:
1. RUNWAY INTERSECTION HOLD WITHOUT SURFACE MARKING: KSFO HS-4 specifically warns that at runway intersections at KSFO, vertical signs exist for holding positions but there are NO surface painted markings. This is a non-standard configuration relative to typical airports where both signs and painted markings exist. If SWA3301's crew expects a painted hold-short marking and sees none, they may assume no hold position exists and continue past the vertical signs.
2. MANDATORY STOP AT VERTICAL SIGN: When ATC issues 'hold position,' SWA3301 must stop at the vertical holding position sign. The sign is the legally binding hold position marker even in the absence of surface paint. The crew must treat the vertical sign exactly as they would treat painted runway holding position markings.

Required action: SWA3301 must reject the takeoff if below V1 and stop at the vertical holding position sign at the runway intersection. This is an ATC-directed stop during takeoff roll — compliance is mandatory.
required_safety_constraints: KSFO HS-4: at runway intersections, vertical holding position signs exist with no surface painted markings — pilots must hold at the signs (FAA Chart Supplement SW, cycle 20260514, FAA_CS_SW_20260514.pdf, p.647)
ATC-directed 'hold position' during takeoff roll requires immediate rejected takeoff if below V1 (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-9-5)
Vertical holding position signs are legally equivalent to painted markings for hold-short compliance (FAA AC 150/5340-1L §2.4)
No surface markings at KSFO runway intersections does not mean no hold position exists — vertical signs are authoritative

prompt:
Airport: KPHX (Phoenix Sky Harbor International). Hot Spot HS-1: 'Rwy 07L and Rwy 07R departures sometimes misidentify Twy F for Rwy 07L or Rwy 07R.'

Situation: Aircraft ASA446 (B737-900) is taxiing outbound for departure on Rwy 07R in pre-dawn darkness. Ground control cleared: 'ASA446, taxi to runway 07R via Taxiway F, hold short of runway 07R.' As ASA446 taxis on Twy F in the dark, the crew has difficulty distinguishing the taxiway surface from the parallel runway surface. Weather: 10SM CLR, wind calm, 05:20 local time (pre-dawn).

Identify the surface movement hazard.
gold_decision:
Critical hazard identified:
1. MISIDENTIFICATION OF TWY F AS RWY 07L OR 07R: KPHX HS-1 specifically warns that departing crews for Rwy 07L and 07R sometimes misidentify Twy F as the runway. In pre-dawn darkness, the distinction between Twy F (taxiway, yellow centerline) and Rwy 07L/07R (runway, white centerline and edge lights) may be visually ambiguous. A crew that mistakes Twy F for the runway may attempt to depart on Twy F — a wrong-surface takeoff attempt — which is an extremely dangerous surface incident.
2. WRONG-SURFACE TAKEOFF RISK: KPHX Rwy 07L is 10,300 ft x 150 ft and Rwy 07R is 7,800 ft x 150 ft. Twy F is narrower and has no runway markings or approach lighting. A crew attempting takeoff on Twy F would encounter different visual cues at rotation — if the error is not caught before V1, a wrong-surface incident occurs on a surface not designed for takeoff.

Required action: ASA446 crew must verify runway identity by: (1) confirming white centerline and edge lighting (runway) vs yellow centerline (taxiway); (2) checking runway number sign at the holding position; (3) confirming compass heading alignment with runway 07R heading (070° magnetic) before entering the runway.
required_safety_constraints: KPHX HS-1: Rwy 07L and 07R departures sometimes misidentify Twy F as the runway (FAA Chart Supplement SW, cycle 20260514, FAA_CS_SW_20260514.pdf, p.644)
Pre-dawn/night operations increase wrong-surface identification risk (FAA Safety Alert for Operators 17007)
Crew must confirm white runway centerline and edge lighting, runway number sign, and compass heading alignment before entering runway for departure (FAA AC 91-73B §3.3)
Wrong-surface takeoff is a catastrophic safety risk — both crew members must independently verify runway identity (FAA Safety Alert for Operators 17007)

prompt:
Airport: KPHX (Phoenix Sky Harbor International). Hot Spot HS-2: 'Pilots sometimes cross Rwy 07L/25R at Twy F8, Twy F9, or Twy F10 without authorization.'

Situation: Aircraft FFT2201 (A319) has been cleared: 'FFT2201, taxi to gate A6 via Taxiway F.' As FFT2201 taxis on Twy F, the crew crosses Twy F8 junction, then Twy F9. ATC has not issued any runway crossing clearance. KPHX Rwy 07L/25R is active with departures on Rwy 07L. ASDE-X is in use.

Identify the surface movement hazard and required action.
gold_decision:
Critical hazard identified:
1. UNAUTHORIZED RUNWAY CROSSING AT F8/F9/F10: KPHX HS-2 specifically warns that pilots taxi on Twy F and cross Rwy 07L/25R at Twy F8, F9, or F10 without authorization. FFT2201 is on Twy F approaching these junction points. If Twy F8, F9, or F10 are taxi routes that cross the active Rwy 07L/25R and FFT2201 continues without a runway crossing clearance, FFT2201 will commit a runway incursion.
2. ASDE-X MONITORING: KPHX uses ASDE-X surface detection equipment. An unauthorized crossing will trigger an ASDE-X alert to ATC, but by the time ATC responds, FFT2201 may already be on the runway surface with active departures present.

Required action: FFT2201 must verify its taxi clearance does not cross Rwy 07L/25R via F8, F9, or F10 without explicit runway crossing authorization. If the assigned route crosses the runway at any of these taxiways, FFT2201 must stop before the runway holding position and await explicit crossing clearance from ATC before proceeding.

Each runway crossing requires explicit ATC authorization — no crossing is implicit in a taxi-to-gate clearance (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2)
ASDE-X in use at KPHX — unauthorized crossing will be detected (FAA Chart Supplement SW, cycle 20260514, FAA_CS_SW_20260514.pdf, p.76)
Aircraft must stop at runway holding position markings and await crossing clearance before proceeding over Rwy 07L/25R (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2)

prompt:
Airport: KDEN (Denver International). Hot Spot HS-1: 'Rwy 17R apch area; hold short when directed by ATC; Pilots turning eastbound onto Twy ED from Twy M sometimes cross Rwy 17R APCH hold bar without authorization.'

Situation: Aircraft UAL1401 (B737-900) is taxiing eastbound on Twy M, turning onto Twy ED. ATC has instructed: 'UAL1401, hold short of runway 17R approach area.' UAL1401 is in IMC (OVC003, RVR 4000). The crew turns from Twy M onto Twy ED and sees a hold bar ahead. An ILS approach is in progress to Rwy 17R.

Identify the surface movement hazards.
gold_decision:
Two critical hazards identified:
1. HOT SPOT HS-1 UNAUTHORIZED APPROACH-AREA CROSSING: KDEN HS-1 specifically warns that eastbound traffic on Twy M turning onto Twy ED sometimes crosses the Rwy 17R approach hold bar without authorization. UAL1401 is executing exactly this maneuver in IMC. The crew must stop at the hold bar and verify the hold-short instruction has been complied with before proceeding.
2. ILS CRITICAL AREA PROTECTION: An ILS approach is in progress to Rwy 17R in IMC (OVC003). The Rwy 17R approach hold bar at the Twy ED/M intersection may be in the ILS critical area or approach protection area. UAL1401 crossing this hold bar would violate ILS approach protection, potentially distorting the localizer or glideslope signal for the inbound aircraft.

Required action: UAL1401 must stop at the approach hold bar, verify position, and not proceed until ATC explicitly authorizes crossing. The combination of HS-1 disorientation risk and active ILS approach in IMC makes this a critical safety scenario.

prompt:
Airport: KORD (Chicago O'Hare International). Hot Spot HS-1: 'Surface incident risk, Twy A between Twy A14 and Twy A12; acft pushing onto Twy A from gates K18 and K20.'

Situation: Aircraft UAL789 (B787-9) is taxiing outbound on Twy A (between A14 and A12). At the same time, a ground crew is pushing back aircraft AAL230 (B737-800) from gate K18 onto Twy A. The pushback has just entered Twy A. KORD Special Air Traffic Rules (Part 93) are in effect. ASDE-X is in use. Weather: 10SM SCT040.

Identify all surface movement hazards.
gold_decision:
Two hazards identified:
1. HOT SPOT HS-1 PUSHBACK CONFLICT: KORD HS-1 specifically identifies pushbacks from gates K18 and K20 onto Twy A between A14 and A12 as a surface incident risk. UAL789 taxiing on Twy A is approaching the K18 pushback zone. The pushback of AAL230 has entered Twy A, creating a conflict with UAL789's forward movement. The pushback operation narrows or blocks the taxiway, requiring UAL789 to stop before reaching the conflict zone.
2. LARGE AIRCRAFT WINGSPAN PROXIMITY: KORD prohibits aircraft with wingspan >214 ft on taxiways without special authorization, and has specific prohibitions on Twy A17 (>96 ft wingspan) and Twy SP (>118 ft wingspan). The B787-9 has a wingspan of ~197 ft, which is within the general limit but the Twy A segment between A14 and A12 must be verified for wingspan restrictions. Pushback aircraft operating in this zone creates additional lateral clearance risk.

Required action: UAL789 must stop clear of the pushback conflict zone and contact Ground Control for instruction. Ground Control must sequence the pushback of AAL230 and the through-movement of UAL789 to avoid simultaneous Twy A occupation between A14 and A12.
required_safety_constraints: KORD HS-1: pushbacks from gates K18 and K20 onto Twy A between A14 and A12 create surface incident risk (FAA Chart Supplement EC, cycle 20260514, FAA_CS_EC_20260514.pdf, p.451)
Aircraft on Twy A must stop clear of pushback zone — cannot push into occupied taxiway (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-1)
KORD ASDE-X in use — ATC has surface detection capability to monitor the conflict (FAA Chart Supplement EC, cycle 20260514, FAA_CS_EC_20260514.pdf, p.43)
KORD Special Air Traffic Rules Part 93 apply — operating transporter with ADS-B required (FAA Chart Supplement EC, cycle 20260514, FAA_CS_EC_20260514.pdf, p.43)

prompt:
Airport: KSEA (Seattle-Tacoma International). Hot Spot HS-1: 'Acft taxiing to Rwy 34C at Twy Q for departure sometimes enter Rwy 34R wo authorization after reading back hold short instructions. Rwy 34R hold position is only 275 ft from the ramp and movement area boundary.'

Situation: Aircraft ASA2101 (B737-900ER) has been cleared: 'ASA2101, taxi to runway 34C via Twy Q, hold short of runway 34R.' The crew reads back correctly: 'Taxi to runway 34C via Q, hold short 34R, ASA2101.' As ASA2101 exits the ramp area and enters Twy Q, the crew is focused on the 34C departure checklist. Rwy 34R hold position is 275 ft from the ramp boundary.

Identify the surface movement hazard.
gold_decision:
Critical hazard identified:
1. HOT SPOT HS-1 PROXIMITY OF RWY 34R HOLD POSITION: KSEA HS-1 identifies that the Rwy 34R hold position on Twy Q is only 275 ft from the ramp boundary. At normal taxi speed (15-20 kts), UAL2101 covers 275 ft in approximately 8-10 seconds of movement from the ramp boundary. With the crew distracted by the departure checklist, there is extremely limited time to recognize and stop at the Rwy 34R hold position before crossing it.
2. CORRECT READ-BACK BUT CHECKLIST DISTRACTION: The crew correctly read back the hold-short instruction. However, the HS-1 pattern specifically notes that crews who read back correctly STILL enter Rwy 34R. This is a checklist/task-saturation distraction scenario. The read-back confirms understanding but does not prevent distraction-induced non-compliance.

Required action: ASA2101 crew must delay or interrupt the departure checklist while on Twy Q to focus exclusively on stopping at the Rwy 34R hold position before the narrow 275-ft window from the ramp is exceeded. Hold the checklist, stop at 34R, then resume checklist.
required_safety_constraints: KSEA HS-1: Rwy 34R hold position at Twy Q is only 275 ft from ramp boundary — extremely short taxi distance to hold position (FAA Chart Supplement NW, cycle 20260514, FAA_CS_NW_20260514.pdf, p.371)
Departure checklist must not distract crew from hold-short compliance at active runways (FAA AC 91-73B §3.1 sterile taxi equivalent)
Crew must be ready to stop within 275 ft of entering Twy Q from the ramp — effectively at minimum taxi speed (FAA AC 91-73B §3.1)
Correct read-back does not guarantee compliance — monitoring is required throughout the taxi (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2)
acceptable_variants: Crew may ask ground to delay taxi clearance until departure checklist is complete, then proceed to 34R hold-short

prompt:
Airport: KSEA (Seattle-Tacoma International). Hot Spot HS-2: 'Acft crossing/exiting Rwy 16C-34C at Twy J sometimes cross Rwy 16L-34R hold line on Twy H wo authorization. Hold line immediately after joining Twy H.'

Situation: Aircraft DAL1602 (A321) has just vacated Rwy 16C and is exiting via Twy J. Ground control cleared: 'DAL1602, exit Rwy 16C via Twy J, proceed to gate N18.' As DAL1602 turns from Twy J onto Twy H, the crew sees the Rwy 16L-34R hold line on Twy H. The hold line is very close to the Twy J/H junction — the HS-2 description states it is 'immediately after joining Twy H.'

Identify the surface movement hazard and required action.
gold_decision:
Critical hazard identified:
1. IMMEDIATE HOLD LINE ON TWY H: KSEA HS-2 warns that the Rwy 16L-34R hold line on Twy H is positioned immediately after an aircraft joins Twy H from Twy J. DAL1602 decelerating from a runway exit, turning onto Twy J, then immediately turning onto Twy H — will encounter the Rwy 16L-34R hold line within seconds of completing the Twy H turn. There is minimal distance between the turn onto Twy H and the hold line.
2. UNAUTHORIZED CROSSING PATTERN: HS-2 documents that aircraft 'sometimes cross the Rwy 16L-34R hold line on Twy H without authorization' — this is a documented incursion pattern at KSEA. DAL1602's clearance (taxi to gate N18) does NOT include an explicit Rwy 16L-34R crossing clearance. If the crew does not stop at the Twy H hold line, they will commit an unauthorized runway crossing.

Required action: DAL1602 must actively anticipate the Rwy 16L-34R hold line on Twy H, slow to near-stop before completing the Twy J/H turn, and stop at the hold line. Do not proceed unless ATC explicitly authorizes crossing Rwy 16L-34R.
required_safety_constraints: KSEA HS-2: Rwy 16L-34R hold line on Twy H is immediately after joining Twy H from Twy J (FAA Chart Supplement NW, cycle 20260514, FAA_CS_NW_20260514.pdf, p.371)
No explicit crossing clearance for Rwy 16L-34R was issued — taxi-to-gate does not authorize runway crossing (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2)
Aircraft must stop at the Rwy 16L-34R hold line on Twy H and await crossing clearance (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2)
Crew must anticipate the hold line BEFORE completing the Twy H turn — reactive stops after a fast turn are insufficient (FAA AC 91-73B §3.1)

prompt:
Airport: KSAN (San Diego International). Hot Spot HS-1: 'Twy J at Twy H — Pilots at GA parking instructed to taxi via Twy H and Twy C; incorrectly turn onto Twy J instead.'

Situation: A Cessna 172 (N1122J, GA aircraft) has been instructed: 'N1122J, taxi to runway 27, via Twy H, Twy C.' The pilot is unfamiliar with KSAN. As N1122J reaches the Twy J/H intersection, the pilot sees Twy J and Twy H diverging. Uncertain of direction, the pilot turns left onto Twy J instead of continuing on Twy H. KSAN is a complex airport with airline and GA traffic mixed.

Identify the surface movement hazard and required action.
gold_decision:
Two hazards identified:
1. HOT SPOT HS-1 WRONG TAXIWAY: KSAN HS-1 specifically documents that GA pilots at this intersection incorrectly turn onto Twy J instead of continuing on Twy H (or Twy C). N1122J has committed exactly this HS-1 error. Twy J leads to a different area of the airport from the intended route, and may bring N1122J into proximity with airline operations or restricted areas near the terminal.
2. SITUATIONAL AWARENESS LOSS: N1122J's pilot, unfamiliar with KSAN, does not realize they have deviated from the assigned taxi route. If the pilot continues on Twy J without reporting position confusion to ATC, they may taxi into unauthorized areas, violate movement area boundaries, or enter a runway without clearance.

Required action: N1122J must stop immediately upon recognizing the turn onto Twy J, report to Ground Control: 'Ground, N1122J, I believe I've turned onto Twy J instead of continuing on Twy H, requesting clarification,' and await ATC instructions before moving.

Pilot must report position uncertainty to ATC immediately — do not continue taxiing when route deviation is recognized (FAA AC 91-73B §3.2)

prompt:
Airport: KORD (Chicago O'Hare International). Airport remark: 'Acft with wingspan greater than 214 ft require 48 hours PPR (prior permission required). Acft with wingspan greater than 96 ft prohibited on Twy A17. Twy SP closed to wingspan >118 ft.'

Situation: A charter airline operates an A380-800 (wingspan ~262 ft) into KORD. The crew has been cleared to push back from gate G8 and taxi to Rwy 10C via Twy A. The crew was not informed of KORD's Special Air Traffic Rules (Part 93) restrictions on large aircraft. ASDE-X is in use. Weather: 10SM CLR.

Identify all surface movement hazards.
gold_decision:
Two critical hazards identified:
1. PPR VIOLATION FOR WINGSPAN >214 FT: KORD requires 48-hour prior permission required (PPR) for any aircraft with wingspan greater than 214 ft. The A380-800 has a wingspan of ~262 ft. If the crew/airline did not obtain PPR before arriving at KORD, the operation is unauthorized from the start. ATC at KORD should not have cleared the A380 for taxi without confirming PPR on file.
2. TAXIWAY ROUTING RESTRICTION: KORD prohibits aircraft with wingspan >214 ft on most narrow taxiways. All Part 91 and charter operators must verify their routing avoids restricted taxiways for the A380's wingspan. Twy A (which the crew is cleared to use) has specific wingspan restrictions — Twy A380-800 constraints exist, and KORD notes specific taxiways cannot be used by A380 class. The crew must verify the taxi routing is cleared for an A380 wingspan before proceeding.

Required action: Stop pushback, verify PPR authorization is on file, confirm taxi routing has been specifically approved for A380 wingspan class, and contact KORD Operations before any movement.
required_safety_constraints: KORD requires 48-hour PPR for aircraft with wingspan >214 ft (FAA Chart Supplement EC, cycle 20260514, FAA_CS_EC_20260514.pdf, p.43)
KORD taxi routing must be pre-approved for A380 wingspan class — standard routings may be restricted (FAA Chart Supplement EC, cycle 20260514, FAA_CS_EC_20260514.pdf, p.43)

KORD Special Air Traffic Rules Part 93 apply — ADS-B required; operate transponders with altitude reporting on all airport surfaces (FAA Chart Supplement EC, cycle 20260514, FAA_CS_EC_20260514.pdf, p.43)

prompt:
Airport: KLGA (LaGuardia, New York). Airport remark: 'Rwy 13-31 NW: 1776 ft of rwy is a deck over water. Rwy 13-31 SE: 944 ft portion is deck over water. EMAS on Rwy 04, 13, 22, 31.'

Situation: Aircraft DAL677 (B717-200) has landed on Rwy 22. Runway 22 has EMAS (Engineered Materials Arresting System) installed at the far end. The aircraft has used most of the available runway for deceleration. Ground speed at midfield is 35 kts. The crew is considering a high-speed exit before the end of the runway to avoid taxiing past the EMAS. Weather: 10SM CLR, wind 220/08.

Identify the surface movement hazard and required action.
gold_decision:
Two hazards identified:
1. EMAS AT RWY 22 END: KLGA has EMAS installed on Rwy 04, 13, 22, and 31. The EMAS on Rwy 22 is at the runway end to arrest aircraft that overrun. A crew considering whether they can stop before the EMAS must recognize that the EMAS begins AT the runway end, not beyond it — the intent is to prevent overruns off the runway end, not to serve as a deceleration device. If the crew cannot stop before the EMAS, they should initiate maximum braking immediately.
2. HIGH-SPEED EXIT RISK: The crew at 35 kts considering a 'high-speed exit' must identify which taxiway exit is available before reaching the EMAS end. KLGA Rwy 22 has limited exit options. A high-speed turn at 35 kts onto a taxiway risks taxiway exceedance and potential damage. Exiting at excessive speed is itself a surface hazard.

Required action: Apply normal/maximum braking as required to ensure stop before EMAS area. Use the earliest safe taxiway exit at a speed appropriate for the turn. Do not attempt a high-speed turn that could result in taxiway exceedance.
required_safety_constraints: KLGA EMAS on Rwy 22 ends at the runway boundary — it is a last-resort overrun arresting system, not part of the normal landing surface (FAA Chart Supplement NE, cycle 20260514, FAA_CS_NE_20260514.pdf, p.228)
Aircraft must not enter EMAS intentionally — it is an emergency overrun system (FAA AC 150/5220-22B)
High-speed taxiway exits must not exceed recommended exit speeds for the specific taxiway geometry (FAA AC 91-73B §3.1)
KLGA Rwy 13-31 has deck-over-water sections — runway surface characteristics differ from full ground-based runways (FAA Chart Supplement NE, cycle 20260514, FAA_CS_NE_20260514.pdf, p.228)

prompt:
Airport: KIAH (George Bush Intercontinental, Houston). Airport remark: 'Twy SW Rwy 09-27 to acft wingspan >125 ft clsd. Twy WA and Twy WB: magnetic anomalies may affect compass hdg.'

Situation: Aircraft UAL502 (B767-400ER, wingspan ~170 ft) is cleared: 'UAL502, taxi to runway 27 via Twy SW.' The crew knows the route and begins taxiing. KIAH ASDE-X and GBAS approach service are in use. Weather: 10SM CLR.

Identify the surface movement hazard and required action.
gold_decision:
Critical hazard identified:
1. TAXIWAY CLOSURE FOR WINGSPAN: KIAH airport remarks state that Twy SW from Rwy 09-27 is closed to aircraft with wingspan greater than 125 ft. The B767-400ER has a wingspan of approximately 170 ft, which exceeds the 125-ft limit. UAL502 taxiing on Twy SW violates this restriction.
2. MAGNETIC COMPASS UNRELIABILITY NEARBY: KIAH notes that Twy WA and Twy WB have magnetic anomalies that may affect compass heading. If UAL502's taxi route passes near these taxiways, the crew should not rely on compass heading for directional reference in those areas — use visual taxiway signs and airport diagram.

Required action: UAL502 must not taxi on Twy SW. The crew must immediately contact Ground Control to report the routing conflict: 'Ground, UAL502, our wingspan is 170 ft — unable Twy SW per KIAH restrictions, requesting alternate routing.' ATC must assign an alternate taxi route to runway 27 that accommodates B767-400ER wingspan.
required_safety_constraints: KIAH Twy SW closed to aircraft with wingspan >125 ft — B767-400ER (170 ft wingspan) is prohibited from using Twy SW (FAA Chart Supplement SC, cycle 20260514, FAA_CS_SC_20260514.pdf, p.358)
Crew must not proceed on a restricted taxiway — stop and request alternate routing immediately (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-1)

KIAH magnetic anomalies on Twy WA and Twy WB affect compass heading — do not use compass for direction reference in these areas (FAA Chart Supplement SC, cycle 20260514, FAA_CS_SC_20260514.pdf, p.358)
KIAH ASDE-X and GBAS in use — ATC has surface detection and will observe any taxiway restriction violation (FAA Chart Supplement SC, cycle 20260514, FAA_CS_SC_20260514.pdf, p.358)
evidence_requirements: FAA Chart Supplement South Central U.S., cycle 20260514, FAA_CS_SC_20260514.pdf, p.358 — KIAH taxiway wingspan restrictions and magnetic anomaly remarks
FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-1 — Taxi clearance conflict and re-routing

prompt:
Airport: KDFW (Dallas/Fort Worth International). Airport note: Runway Status Lights (RWSL) system installed. GND CON frequencies: 121.65, 121.8 (East), 121.85 (West).

Situation: Aircraft AAL1601 (B737-MAX8) is holding short of Rwy 35C on Twy EJ. Runway Status Lights (RWSL) at the Twy EJ holding position are LIT RED. Tower frequency shows active departure traffic on Rwy 35C. The ground controller has just issued: 'AAL1601, cross runway 35C.' AAL1601's crew acknowledges 'Cross runway 35C, AAL1601.' The Runway Status Lights remain red.

Identify the surface movement hazard and required action.
gold_decision:
Critical hazard identified:
1. CONFLICTING RUNWAY STATUS LIGHTS: KDFW has Runway Status Lights installed. The RWSL at Twy EJ are LIT RED, indicating runway activity detected by the automated system (departure traffic on Rwy 35C). The crew has received an ATC crossing clearance, but the RWSL are still red.
2. RWSL CONFLICT WITH ATC CLEARANCE: Runway Status Lights are an independent automated safety system. When RWSL are red, they indicate a safety conflict even if an ATC clearance has been issued. FAA policy: if RWSL are red, the aircraft must NOT cross the runway, even with an ATC crossing clearance. The crew must hold and inform ATC that RWSL are red.

Required action: AAL1601 must hold short of Rwy 35C despite the crossing clearance. Inform ATC: 'AAL1601 holding, runway status lights are red at EJ.' Wait for the RWSL to extinguish before proceeding. ATC should verify runway clearance and re-issue the crossing clearance after RWSL extinguish.
required_safety_constraints: Runway Status Lights (RWSL) are an independent safety system — lit red RWSL override an ATC crossing clearance; do not cross (FAA AIM §2-1-14)
AAL1601 must hold and notify ATC of lit RWSL before crossing (FAA AIM §2-1-14)
KDFW has RWSL installed — crews must brief RWSL procedures before taxiing at KDFW (FAA Chart Supplement SC, cycle 20260514, FAA_CS_SC_20260514.pdf, p.309)
RWSL cannot be overridden by verbal ATC clearance — the automated system has an independent authority for runway safety (FAA AIM §2-1-14)
evidence_requirements: FAA Chart Supplement South Central U.S., cycle 20260514, FAA_CS_SC_20260514.pdf, p.309 — KDFW airport (Runway Status Lights)
FAA AIM §2-1-14 — Runway Status Lights operational procedures

prompt:
Airport: KJFK (John F. Kennedy International). Airport remark: 'Twy VA clsd between Trml 7 apron and Twy A. Ufn Twy D between Twy C and hangar 7 clsd.'

Situation: Aircraft BRT1201 (B777-300ER) is cleared to taxi from Terminal 7 apron to the cargo area via Twy VA to Twy A. The flight crew was briefed at dispatch that Twy VA is available. ASDE-X is in use. Weather: 10SM CLR, wind 040/12.

Identify the surface movement hazard.
gold_decision:
Critical hazard identified:
1. CLOSED TAXIWAY: KJFK airport remarks state that Twy VA is closed between Terminal 7 apron and Twy A. The crew's dispatch briefing indicated Twy VA was available — this information is outdated or incorrect. If the crew taxis onto Twy VA between Terminal 7 apron and Twy A, they will be on a closed taxiway. Closed taxiways may have equipment, construction, or other hazards on the surface, and the aircraft may not be able to maneuver or reverse safely.
2. ASDE-X DETECTION: KJFK ASDE-X will detect the aircraft on a closed taxiway and ATC will issue a traffic alert. However, by the time ATC alerts the crew, the aircraft may be committed on the closed taxiway.

Required action: Before taxi, crew must cross-check NOTAM file for KJFK (NOTAM FILE ORD is referenced in KJFK — actually KJFK uses its own NOTAM) and verify Twy VA status from current Chart Supplement and active NOTAMs. If Twy VA is confirmed closed, request alternate routing before taxi.
required_safety_constraints: KJFK Twy VA closed between Terminal 7 apron and Twy A (FAA Chart Supplement NE, cycle 20260514, FAA_CS_NE_20260514.pdf, p.226)
Crew must verify taxiway closures via NOTAMs and current Chart Supplement before accepting taxi routing over closed taxiways (FAA AC 91-73B §3.4)
Twy D between Twy C and hangar 7 also closed at KJFK (FAA Chart Supplement NE, cycle 20260514, FAA_CS_NE_20260514.pdf, p.226)

ASDE-X in use at KJFK — ATC will detect movement on closed taxiway but crew must not rely on ATC detection to prevent this error (FAA Chart Supplement NE, cycle 20260514, FAA_CS_NE_20260514.pdf, p.226)

prompt:
Airport: KSFO (San Francisco International). Hot Spot HS-1: 'Complex intersections close proximity of rwys; Twy C sometimes turn onto Twy F instead of continuing turn on Twy B.'

Situation: Aircraft UAL445 (B737-900) is cleared: 'UAL445, taxi via Twy C, Twy B, hold short of Rwy 28L.' As UAL445 is taxiing on Twy C and approaching the Twy B junction, the crew sees both Twy B and Twy F as possible turn options. Twy F leads in a direction that superficially resembles the intended route. Weather: 3SM -DZ OVC010 (reduced visibility).

Identify the surface movement hazard.
gold_decision:
Two hazards identified:
1. HOT SPOT HS-1 WRONG TAXIWAY TURN: KSFO HS-1 specifically warns that aircraft on Twy C sometimes turn onto Twy F instead of continuing the turn onto Twy B. In 3SM drizzle/overcast 1000ft conditions, visual distinction between Twy B and Twy F is reduced. UAL445's crew is at exactly this known confusion point. Turning onto Twy F instead of Twy B takes the aircraft onto the wrong taxiway, diverging from the intended route and potentially bringing UAL445 close to runway areas not cleared for crossing.
2. RUNWAY PROXIMITY: KSFO HS-1 notes complex intersections and close runway proximity. An incorrect Twy F turn in reduced visibility could bring UAL445 toward a runway without the crew realizing they are on the wrong taxiway, given the degraded visual environment.

Required action: UAL445 crew must slow at the Twy B/F junction, reference KSFO airport diagram explicitly to identify Twy B vs Twy F, and confirm the correct turn before proceeding. If uncertain, stop and contact ATC.
required_safety_constraints: KSFO HS-1: aircraft on Twy C sometimes turn onto Twy F instead of Twy B (FAA Chart Supplement SW, cycle 20260514, FAA_CS_SW_20260514.pdf, p.647)
Reduced visibility (3SM/-DZ/OVC010) elevates taxiway identification error risk — airport diagram reference is mandatory at junctions (FAA AC 91-73B §3.4)
Aircraft must stop and contact ATC if unable to positively identify correct taxiway (FAA AC 91-73B §3.2)
EMAS installed on multiple runways at KSFO — active runway proximity risk is elevated (FAA Chart Supplement SW, cycle 20260514, FAA_CS_SW_20260514.pdf, p.275)

prompt:
Airport: KATL (Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International). Ground Communications: 'GND CON 125.65 (Rwy 09L-27R/09R-27L), 133.475 (Rwy 08L-26R/08R-26L), 121.65 (Rwy 10-28).'

Situation: Aircraft DAL2201 (B737-900) has just landed on Rwy 26R and is taxiing inbound. The crew contacts KATL Ground on 121.65, which is the Rwy 10-28 ground frequency. The 26R arrival is on the Rwy 08L-26R/08R-26L complex, which uses ground frequency 133.475. The crew does not receive any response and waits 30 seconds. An intersecting path with another aircraft taxiing under control of 133.475 is approaching.

Identify the surface movement hazard and required action.
gold_decision:
Two hazards identified:
1. WRONG GROUND FREQUENCY: KATL has three separate ground control frequencies assigned by runway complex: 121.65 for Rwy 10-28, 125.65 for Rwy 09L-27R and 09R-27L, and 133.475 for Rwy 08L-26R and 08R-26L. DAL2201 landed on Rwy 26R and is in the 133.475 ground control area. By contacting 121.65, the crew is on the wrong frequency and ATC cannot issue taxi instructions to DAL2201 on the other frequency.
2. UNCONTROLLED INTERSECTION APPROACH: With DAL2201 on the wrong frequency and receiving no instructions, an intersecting aircraft under 133.475 control is approaching. ATC controlling the intersecting aircraft cannot issue safety instructions to DAL2201 because they are on different frequencies. This creates an uncoordinated surface conflict.

Required action: DAL2201 must immediately try 133.475 (the correct KATL GND frequency for Rwy 08L-26R operations), contact ATC, report position, and await taxi instructions before proceeding to any intersection.
required_safety_constraints: KATL uses frequency-split ground control: 133.475 for Rwy 08L-26R/08R-26L area (FAA Chart Supplement SE, cycle 20260514, FAA_CS_SE_20260514.pdf, p.204)
Operating on the wrong ground frequency leaves aircraft uncontrolled and creates potential surface conflicts at KATL's complex multiple-runway layout (FAA AC 91-73B §3.3)
Crew must verify correct ground frequency before taxiing at multi-frequency airports — ATIS and airport diagram list frequency assignments (FAA AC 91-73B §3.3)
If no ATC response received, crew must try alternate frequencies before assuming clearance to proceed (FAA AC 91-73B §3.2)
evidence_requirements: FAA Chart Supplement Southeast U.S., cycle 20260514, FAA_CS_SE_20260514.pdf, p.204 — KATL GND CON frequency assignments
FAA AC 91-73B §3.3 — Communications procedures and frequency management

prompt: You are reviewing the depicted FAA airport diagram for KSAT. ATC clears the aircraft to 'cross runway 30R at A' but the airport has multiple intersecting runways near A. Identify all relevant surface-movement safety hazards. Cite specific runway/taxiway elements visible on the diagram.
gold_decision: Hazards: (1) potential mis-identification of which runway to cross when multiple runways intersect at the same taxiway intersection; (2) confirm the cleared runway designator matches the painted runway designator on the surface; (3) NOTAM or hot-spot for the crossing point must be checked before entering.
required_safety_constraints: Pilot must positively identify runway designator before crossing
Reject takeoff/crossing if any ambiguity remains

prompt: You are reviewing the depicted FAA airport diagram for KSEA. Night operations, taxi clearance 'taxi to runway 34R via F, expect departure 16L'. Identify all relevant surface-movement safety hazards. Cite specific runway/taxiway elements visible on the diagram.
gold_decision: Hazards at night: (1) reduced visual references — depend on centerline / edge lighting; (2) easy to follow incorrect taxiway if intersections are complex; (3) any unlit areas on the airport diagram (NOTAM'd outages) must be verified before entering.
required_safety_constraints: Verify lit centerline matches cleared taxi route
Check NOTAM for any lighting outage along the route
